news_summary: A remarkable discovery reveals that cows can use tools similarly to primates, showcasing impressive animal intelligence and advancing our understanding of animal behavior.
news_summary_fr: Une découverte remarquable révèle que les vaches sont capables d'utiliser des outils à l'instar des primates, ce qui met en évidence leur intelligence impressionnante et nous permet de mieux comprendre leur comportement.
news_summary_es: Un descubrimiento sorprendente revela que las vacas pueden utilizar herramientas de forma similar a los primates, lo que pone de manifiesto su impresionante inteligencia y amplía nuestro conocimiento sobre el comportamiento animal.

articles.title: This cow uses tools like a primate—and scientists are stunned
articles.summary: A cow named Veronika has stunned scientists by using tools in a flexible and purposeful way. She chooses different ends of a brush depending on the part of her body and adjusts her movements accordingly. This level of tool use is incredibly rare and was previously seen mainly in primates. The finding hints that cows may be much smarter than we assume.
articles.content:
In 1982, cartoonist Gary Larson introduced a famous Far Side comic called Cow Tools. The illustration showed a cow proudly standing next to a strange collection of useless objects labeled as tools. The humor relied on a widely accepted idea that cows lack the intelligence to create or use tools.

That assumption is now being questioned thanks to a real cow named Veronika. A study published in Current Biology reports the first documented case of tool use in a pet cow, suggesting that cattle may be far more cognitively capable than previously believed.

"The findings highlight how assumptions about livestock intelligence may reflect gaps in observation rather than genuine cognitive limits," says Alice Auersperg, a cognitive biologist at the University of Veterinary Medicine, Vienna.

Meet Veronika, the Tool-Using Cow

Veronika is a Swiss Brown cow who lives as a companion animal rather than being raised for food production. She belongs to Witgar Wiegele, an organic farmer and baker who considers her part of the family.

More than a decade ago, Wiegele noticed an unusual behavior. Veronika would pick up sticks and use them to scratch her body. This behavior eventually caught scientific attention when it was recorded on video and shared with researchers.

"When I saw the footage, it was immediately clear that this was not accidental," Auersperg says. "This was a meaningful example of tool use in a species that is rarely considered from a cognitive perspective."

Testing Tool Use in Controlled Experiments

To better understand this behavior, researchers conducted structured tests with Veronika. They placed a deck brush on the ground in different positions and observed how she interacted with it.

Across multiple trials, Veronika consistently chose specific parts of the brush depending on where she wanted to scratch. Her selections were not random. Instead, they matched the needs of different areas of her body.

"We show that a cow can engage in genuinely flexible tool use," says Antonio Osuna-Mascaró. "Veronika is not just using an object to scratch herself. She uses different parts of the same tool for different purposes, and she applies different techniques depending on the function of the tool and the body region."

Flexible and Multi-Purpose Tool Use

The researchers found clear patterns in how Veronika used the brush. For larger, firmer areas like her back, she preferred the bristled side. For more sensitive regions on her lower body, she switched to the smoother handle.

She also adjusted her movements. Scratching her upper body involved broader, stronger motions, while movements directed at lower areas were slower and more precise.

Tool use is defined as using an external object to achieve a goal through physical interaction. Veronika's actions meet this definition and go beyond it. Her behavior qualifies as flexible, multi-purpose tool use, meaning she uses different features of the same object for different outcomes.

This type of behavior is extremely rare and has previously been clearly documented only in chimpanzees among non-human species.

Overcoming Physical Limitations

Veronika's tool use is directed at her own body, which is known as egocentric tool use. While this form is typically considered less complex than using tools on external objects, it still presents challenges.

Because cows lack hands, Veronika must manipulate tools using her mouth. Despite this limitation, she shows careful control and appears to anticipate the effects of her actions. She adjusts her grip and movements to achieve the desired result.

Why This Behavior May Be Rare

The researchers believe Veronika's unique living conditions may have contributed to her behavior. Unlike most cattle, she has lived a long life in a complex and stimulating environment. She has daily interactions with humans and access to a variety of objects she can manipulate.

These factors likely created opportunities for exploration and innovation that are uncommon for most cows.

"[Veronika] did not fashion tools like the cow in Gary Larson's cartoon, but she selected, adjusted, and used one with notable dexterity and flexibility," the researchers write. "Perhaps the real absurdity lies not in imagining a tool-using cow, but in assuming such a thing could never exist."

Rethinking Animal Intelligence

This discovery represents the first confirmed case of tool use in cattle and expands the range of species known to demonstrate this ability. It also raises the possibility that similar behaviors may exist but have gone unnoticed.

The research team is now exploring which environmental and social conditions allow such behaviors to develop. They also encourage others to report similar observations.

"Because we suspect this ability may be more widespread than currently documented," Osuna-Mascaró says, "we invite readers who have observed cows or bulls using sticks or other handheld objects for purposeful actions to contact us."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a scientifically documented breakthrough in animal cognition, showing a cow using tools flexibly and purposefully, which challenges existing assumptions about livestock intelligence. This discovery has broad significance for understanding animal intelligence and welfare, and it is well-supported by detailed observations and experiments. The story focuses on a single, meaningful topic with clear real-world impact in animal cognition and welfare.
articles.personality_title: Cow Veronika shows surprising skill in using tools like primates
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For a long time, people thought cows were not smart enough to use tools. This idea came from jokes and cartoons, like a famous 1982 comic that showed a cow with strange objects it called tools. Scientists believed tool use was mainly a skill of primates, like monkeys and chimpanzees.

**What happened** – A Swiss Brown cow named Veronika surprised researchers by using a brush as a tool to scratch different parts of her body. She chooses which end of the brush to use and changes how she moves it depending on where she wants to scratch. Scientists watched her carefully and tested her with different brush positions. They found she used the tool in a flexible and purposeful way.

**Impact** – This is the first clear example of tool use in cows. Veronika’s behavior shows that cows may be smarter than people thought. She uses different parts of the same tool for different jobs, which is rare outside of primates. This discovery challenges old ideas about animal intelligence and suggests cows can solve problems in creative ways.

**What's next step** – Researchers want to learn more about what helps cows develop these skills. They believe Veronika’s special environment, with lots of objects and human care, helped her. Scientists are asking others to share if they see similar tool use in cows. This could help us understand how animals learn and think.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Veronika the cow proves that cows can use tools thoughtfully, showing surprising intelligence once thought unique to primates.
articles.personality_title_fr: La vache Veronika utilise des outils avec une habileté surprenante, comme les primates
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Pendant longtemps, on a cru que les vaches n’étaient pas assez intelligentes pour utiliser des outils. Cette idée vient de blagues et de dessins animés, comme une célèbre bande dessinée de 1982 montrant une vache avec des objets étranges appelés outils. Les scientifiques pensaient que l’usage d’outils était surtout un talent des primates, comme les singes et chimpanzés.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une vache suisse brune nommée Veronika a surpris les chercheurs en utilisant une brosse comme outil pour se gratter différentes parties de son corps. Elle choisit quelle extrémité de la brosse utiliser et adapte ses mouvements selon la zone à gratter. Les scientifiques l’ont observée attentivement et ont testé son comportement avec la brosse placée différemment. Ils ont découvert qu’elle utilisait l’outil de façon flexible et réfléchie.

**Impact** – C’est la première preuve claire d’utilisation d’outils chez une vache. Le comportement de Veronika montre que les vaches sont peut-être plus intelligentes qu’on le pensait. Elle utilise différentes parties d’un même outil pour différentes fonctions, ce qui est rare en dehors des primates. Cette découverte remet en cause les idées reçues sur l’intelligence animale et suggère que les vaches peuvent résoudre des problèmes de façon créative.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent mieux comprendre ce qui aide les vaches à développer ces compétences. Ils pensent que l’environnement spécial de Veronika, avec beaucoup d’objets et des soins humains, a favorisé son comportement. Les scientifiques invitent d’autres personnes à signaler si elles observent un usage similaire d’outils chez les vaches. Cela pourrait aider à mieux comprendre comment les animaux apprennent et pensent.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Veronika la vache prouve que les vaches peuvent utiliser des outils avec réflexion, montrant une intelligence surprenante avant réservée aux primates.
articles.personality_title_es: La vaca Veronika muestra sorprendente habilidad para usar herramientas como los primates
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante mucho tiempo, se pensó que las vacas no eran lo suficientemente inteligentes para usar herramientas. Esta idea viene de bromas y caricaturas, como una famosa historieta de 1982 que mostraba a una vaca con objetos extraños llamados herramientas. Los científicos creían que el uso de herramientas era principalmente una habilidad de los primates, como monos y chimpancés.

**Qué pasó** – Una vaca suiza llamada Veronika sorprendió a los investigadores al usar un cepillo como herramienta para rascarse diferentes partes de su cuerpo. Ella elige qué extremo del cepillo usar y cambia sus movimientos según la zona que quiere rascar. Los científicos la observaron con atención y la probaron con el cepillo en distintas posiciones. Descubrieron que usaba la herramienta de manera flexible y con intención.

**Impacto** – Este es el primer caso claro de uso de herramientas en vacas. El comportamiento de Veronika muestra que las vacas pueden ser más inteligentes de lo que se pensaba. Ella usa diferentes partes de la misma herramienta para distintos fines, algo raro fuera de los primates. Este hallazgo desafía las ideas antiguas sobre la inteligencia animal y sugiere que las vacas pueden resolver problemas de forma creativa.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores quieren saber más sobre qué ayuda a las vacas a desarrollar estas habilidades. Creen que el entorno especial de Veronika, con muchos objetos y cuidado humano, influyó en su conducta. Los científicos piden a otras personas que compartan si ven un uso similar de herramientas en vacas. Esto podría ayudar a entender mejor cómo aprenden y piensan los animales.

**Resumen en una frase** – Veronika, la vaca, demuestra que las vacas pueden usar herramientas con intención, mostrando una inteligencia sorprendente antes atribuida solo a primates.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a gentle Swiss Brown cow using its mouth to skillfully hold and scratch itself with a deck brush, showing the cow’s focused expression and careful movements, set against a simple, natural farm background with soft earthy tones.
